questionText: A two-digit number has tens digit $a$ and units digit $b$. Prove that the sum of this number and the number formed by reversing its digits is divisible by $11$.

steps.description: Express the two-digit number using place value.
steps.explanation: A two-digit number is $10 \times (\text{tens digit}) + (\text{units digit})$, so it equals $10a+b$. Place value turns the digits into algebra.
Reversing swaps the roles of the digits, so the tens digit becomes $b$ and the units digit becomes $a$, giving $10b+a$.
The statement is about their sum, so we add the original and its reverse.
The $a$ terms give $10a+a=11a$ and the $b$ terms give $b+10b=11b$.
Both terms share a factor of $11$; pulling it out exposes the divisibility we want.

steps.description: Identify the bracket as an integer.
steps.explanation: Digits are integers, so $a+b$ is an integer, making the sum $11 \times (\text{integer})$.
The sum equals $11(a+b)$, a multiple of $11$, so a two-digit number plus its reverse is always divisible by $11$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $(10a+b)+(10b+a)=11a+11b=11(a+b)$, a multiple of $11$. Hence the number plus its reverse is divisible by $11$. $\blacksquare$
